sound_type: shrill bark / chitter -- an aggressive, high-pitched bark combined with rapid chittering, the 68th acoustic element, higher-pitched and more frantic than the Mink's chitter, the smallest carnivore's most desperate and disproportionate vocalization
ecosystem: meadow-forest edge -- the boundary between open meadow and closed forest, field margins, brushy clearings alongside forest, hedgerows, ecotone habitat, the engine's first edge-habitat species
season: year-round resident throughout Washington State, from sea level to alpine

coordinates.description: Reference for meadow-forest edge habitat in the southern Puget Sound lowlands. Short-tailed Weasels are widespread across Washington from sea level to alpine, inhabiting the transitional zones where meadow meets forest, field margins, brushy clearings, and edge habitats of all kinds. Vocalizations most commonly heard during territorial encounters and alarm situations.

taxonomy_note.description: Eighth mammalian entry. Sixth mammalian species. Sixth Carnivora, fifth Mustelidae. FIFTH CONSECUTIVE MUSTELIDAE -- the Mustelidae QUINTET (Otter 63, Mink 64, Marten 65, Fisher 66, Weasel 67). Mustela erminea -- the genus Mustela is the foundational genus of the entire family Mustelidae. The weasel IS the type animal from which the family is named. Also known as Stoat (British) or Ermine (winter coat). Some recent molecular studies suggest North American populations may warrant recognition as Mustela richardsonii, but this revision is not universally adopted.
taxonomy_note.name_etymology: Mustela from Latin mustela, meaning weasel -- the root word from which the entire family Mustelidae derives its name. The weasel is the type animal. erminea from 'ermine,' referring to the white winter fur historically used for royal and ceremonial robes. Common name 'Short-tailed Weasel' distinguishes it from the Long-tailed Weasel (Neogale frenata). 'Stoat' is the British common name, from Middle English stote. 'Ermine' refers specifically to the white winter pelage.
taxonomy_note.genus_note: Mustela -- the foundational genus of Mustelidae. The family name literally means 'of the weasel.' This is the only quintet member in the genus for which the family is named. Unlike the other quintet members (Lontra, Neogale, Martes, Pekania), Mustela was never reclassified because it IS the original, the anchor from which all other mustelid taxonomy radiates.
taxonomy_note.order_note: Carnivora -- sixth entry. All six mammalian Carnivora entries are Mustelidae (Otter 63, Mink 64, Marten 65, Fisher 66, Weasel 67, plus the Spotted Owl's predatory role at degree 4). The order's entire mammalian representation comes from a single family. No non-mustelid mammalian carnivore has entered the engine.
taxonomy_note.class_note: Mammalia -- eighth mammalian entry. Sixth mammalian species. Mammalian representation rises to 11.8% (8 of 68 entries). The mammalian era (degrees 61-67) is now seven degrees deep. Two orders (Rodentia, Carnivora), three families (Castoridae, Mustelidae).
taxonomy_note.family_position: Mustelidae's fifth entry at degree 67. The QUINTET: Otter (63), Mink (64), Marten (65), Fisher (66), Weasel (67). Five consecutive entries -- the engine's deepest family exploration, matching the Swallow Quintet (Hirundinidae 26-30). Mustelidae now represents 5 of 8 PNW mustelid species (62.5% coverage). Nearly two-thirds of Washington's mustelid diversity documented.

song_metrics.fundamental_frequency_range_hz: Variable, estimated 2,000-8,000 Hz for the bark, with chittering components extending higher. The small body and vocal apparatus produce frequencies higher than any other mustelid in the quintet.
song_metrics.peak_energy_band_hz: 3,000-6,000 (the band most characteristic of the shrill quality)
song_metrics.song_frequency_hz: NOT a song but a BARK and CHITTER. A shrill, explosive bark produced during territorial encounters, alarm, and aggressive defense, combined with rapid staccato chittering. Additional vocalizations: squealing (distress), trilling (mating interactions, rare), hissing (close-range defense). The bark and chitter dominate -- the sound of metabolic urgency and territorial aggression.
song_metrics.song_duration_s: Individual barks: 0.1-0.3 seconds. Chitter sequences: 1-5 seconds. Total vocal events during encounters may last 10-60 seconds with pauses.
song_metrics.note_structure: EXPLOSIVE BARK: Short, sharp, high-pitched bursts. Unlike the Fisher's sustained scream or the Marten's screech, the weasel's bark is percussive -- a staccato explosion rather than a held note. RAPID CHITTER: Fast sequences of high-pitched notes, similar to the Mink's chitter but faster and higher-pitched.
song_metrics.note_types: Bark (shrill, explosive, alarm/aggression -- dominant and diagnostic), chitter (rapid staccato sequence, accompanies bark), squeal (distress, high-pitched sustained), trill (mating contact, soft, rare), hiss (close-range defense). The bark is the weasel's signature.
song_metrics.phrase_structure: Bark-chitter combinations. Single barks or bark series followed by chittering. No complex phrase variation. The vocal display is simple but intense -- fury without subtlety.
song_metrics.timbre: Shrill, high-pitched, aggressive. Higher-pitched and more frantic than the Mink's chitter (the most similar vocalization in the quintet). The smaller body produces higher frequencies. The overall quality is of an animal in a state of extreme agitation -- disproportionately loud and aggressive for its size.
song_metrics.amplitude: E=4. Not as loud as the Fisher's E=5 scream, but more frequently heard and delivered with frantic intensity. The E=4 rating reflects the acoustic impact of the bark when produced -- startling, aggressive, disproportionate to the 150-350g body that produces it.
song_metrics.tempo: Fast for a mustelid vocalization. Where the Fisher's scream is sustained and deliberate, the weasel's bark/chitter is rapid and explosive. The Mink's chitter is fast; the weasel's is faster. Everything about the weasel runs at a higher tempo -- metabolic rate, heartbeat, vocalization rate.
song_metrics.context: ALARM/AGGRESSIVE. Produced during territorial encounters, when cornered by predators, during fights with conspecifics, and when defending cached prey. The bark/chitter is both defensive and offensive -- the weasel does not distinguish between flight and fight in its vocal behavior. Also heard when the weasel encounters humans at close range.
song_metrics.regional_variation: NO documented regional variation in vocalizations. The species is widespread and acoustically consistent across its range.
song_metrics.key_property: THE SHRILL BARK / CHITTER -- the 68th acoustic element. Aggressive, high-pitched, frantic. E=4. The smallest carnivore's most desperate sound. Higher-pitched and faster than the Mink's chitter (degree 64). Disproportionate to the animal's size -- a 200-gram predator screaming defiance.

physical_description.overall: Extremely small, elongated mustelid, 170-330 mm total length, 150-350 g. The smallest true carnivore in the engine. Extreme sexual dimorphism -- males roughly twice the weight of females. The most elongated body-to-leg ratio of any mustelid in the quintet. Built for burrow pursuit -- a tube of muscle with teeth.
physical_description.diagnostic_mark: For degree 67, the diagnostic features are: (1) TINY SIZE -- dramatically smaller than all other quintet members. (2) ELONGATED TUBULAR BODY -- the most extreme length-to-width ratio of any mustelid. (3) SUMMER: brown dorsal with sharply demarcated white underparts. (4) WINTER: entirely white (ermine) except black tail tip. (5) BLACK TAIL TIP -- persists year-round, the species' most consistent field mark.
physical_description.fur_detail: SUMMER: Rich chocolate brown dorsal surface with sharply demarcated white to cream underparts. The color boundary is clean and straight. WINTER: Pure white except for the black tail tip. The molt is triggered by photoperiod. In the PNW, completeness varies with latitude and elevation -- mountain weasels turn fully white; coastal lowland weasels may remain partially brown. The ermine (white winter fur) was historically used for coronation robes and heraldic insignia.
physical_description.dental_detail: Carnassial teeth typical of Carnivora but proportionally smaller. 34 teeth total. Strong jaw relative to body size. The killing technique is the mustelid standard: bite to the base of the skull, severing the spinal cord. Effective against prey up to several times the weasel's own body weight.
physical_description.field_id: In edge habitat: a very small, elongated brown (summer) or white (winter) animal with short legs, moving in a bounding gait. In summer, look for the sharp brown/white boundary and the black tail tip. In winter, look for a white animal with a black tail tip against snow. Movement is rapid, low to the ground, with a characteristic bounding lope. More commonly seen than larger mustelids -- weasels are active during daylight hours more than Fishers or Martens. The black tail tip is the single most reliable field mark in all seasons.

nodes.title: The Mustelidae Quintet -- Five Consecutive (63-64-65-66-67)
nodes.description: Five consecutive Mustelidae: Otter (river/estuary, 5-14 kg, social) -> Mink (creek bank, 0.5-1.5 kg, solitary) -> Marten (old-growth canopy, 0.5-1.2 kg, arboreal) -> Fisher (dense forest, 2-6 kg, terrestrial) -> Weasel (meadow-forest edge, 150-350 g, terrestrial/subterranean). The engine's deepest family exploration. Five species, five habitats, five sizes, five acoustic elements. Matches the Swallow Quintet (Hirundinidae 26-30). The quintet traces an arc from water through forest interior and back out to the edge.

nodes.title: The Shrill Bark / Chitter -- 68th Acoustic Element
nodes.description: Shrill, aggressive bark combined with rapid chittering. E=4. Higher-pitched and more frantic than the Mink's chitter (degree 64). The smallest carnivore's most desperate sound -- 150-350 grams of metabolic fury made audible. Startling and disproportionate to the animal's size. The weasel barks defiance at threats many times its weight.

nodes.title: The Ermine Transformation -- Seasonal Color Change
nodes.description: The only species in the engine with a complete seasonal color change. Brown dorsal with white underparts in summer; entirely white except black tail tip in winter. Triggered by photoperiod. In the PNW, completeness varies with latitude and elevation. The white winter coat = ermine, historically the fur of royalty -- coronation robes, heraldic insignia, judicial garments. A 200-gram predator's winter coat dressed kings. No precedent in 67 previous degrees.

nodes.title: Meadow-Forest Edge -- First Edge-Habitat Species
nodes.description: The boundary between open meadow and closed forest. Not meadow, not forest -- the EDGE itself. Field margins, brushy clearings, hedgerows, the ecotone where one habitat grades into another. The engine's first species defined by the transition zone between ecosystems rather than by either ecosystem alone. A genuinely new habitat concept. Edge habitat INCREASES with forest fragmentation -- the same process that destroys Marten and Fisher habitat creates weasel habitat.

nodes.title: Smallest True Carnivore -- The Hypercarnivore
nodes.description: At 150-350 grams, the smallest true carnivore in the engine. Must eat 40-60% of body weight daily -- the most extreme metabolic demand of any species documented. The elongated, tubular body (adapted for burrow pursuit) has an extreme surface-area-to-volume ratio, losing heat rapidly, driving the highest mass-specific metabolic rate. The weasel cannot afford to stop hunting. Metabolic urgency is the foundation of the weasel's entire behavioral repertoire.

nodes.title: Surplus Killing -- Second Instance in the Quintet
nodes.description: Like the Mink (degree 64), the weasel kills more than immediately needed and caches excess prey. But the drivers differ: the Mink surplus-kills from opportunity (prey-dense creek banks), the Weasel from metabolic necessity (40-60% body weight daily). Same behavior, different pressures. Two surplus killers from the same family, three degrees apart. The weasel's surplus killing is insurance against starvation -- it cannot afford to miss a future meal.

nodes.title: Water-to-Edge Arc Across the Quintet
nodes.description: Otter (63): river/estuary, aquatic. Mink (64): creek bank, semi-aquatic margin. Marten (65): old-growth canopy, forest/arboreal. Fisher (66): dense forest, ground/midstory. Weasel (67): meadow-forest edge, semi-open boundary. The quintet traces an ARC, not a line: water -> margin -> canopy -> deep forest -> edge. It goes INTO the forest and then comes back OUT. The weasel reverses the inward spiral -- the first mustelid that lives in the open, at the boundary.

nodes.title: The Conservation Paradox -- Thriving Where Relatives Decline
nodes.description: Edge habitat INCREASES when forests are fragmented. The same activities that eliminated the Fisher (logging, development) and threaten the Marten (old-growth loss) CREATE more habitat for the Short-tailed Weasel. The smallest and simplest member of the quintet is the most resilient. Five mustelids, five conservation stories: recovered (Otter), invasive elsewhere (Mink), old-growth dependent (Marten), reintroduced (Fisher), common and thriving (Weasel).

nodes.title: The Black Tail Tip -- Predator Deflection
nodes.description: The black tail tip persists year-round in both summer and winter pelage. It is the species' most consistent field mark and may function as a predator-deflection decoy: raptors strike at the dark tip against white snow instead of the body, allowing the weasel to escape. Experimental evidence supports this hypothesis. The small dark mark that saves the weasel's life is the same mark that defined heraldic ermine -- the pattern of black spots on white.

nodes.title: Mink-Weasel Acoustic Pair (64 vs. 67)
nodes.description: The two most similar acoustic elements in the quintet. Both are shrill chittering/chattering vocalizations from small, solitary, fierce mustelids. The Weasel's bark/chitter is higher-pitched (smaller body), faster (higher metabolic rate), and more frantic (greater urgency). The Mink is measured in its aggression; the Weasel is unhinged. Creek bank vs. meadow edge. E=3 (Mink) vs. E=4 (Weasel). The smaller the mustelid, the faster and more desperate the sound.

nodes.title: Five Entries, Five Genera -- The Mustela Anchor
nodes.description: Lontra (63), Neogale (64), Martes (65), Pekania (66), Mustela (67). Five quintet members, five different genera. Three reclassified (Otter, Mink, Fisher), two original (Marten, Weasel). The Weasel is in Mustela -- the genus from which the entire family Mustelidae is named. The quintet ends at the beginning: the root genus, the type animal, the etymological anchor of the family.

nodes.title: Climate-Change Signal -- The Ermine Mismatch
nodes.description: The ermine transformation is triggered by photoperiod, not snow cover. As PNW winters become milder and shorter due to climate change, weasels may turn white in environments with no snow -- white on brown ground is visible, not cryptic. The same adaptation that provided survival advantage for millennia may become a liability. The ermine mismatch is a climate-change vulnerability indicator that the engine has not previously documented.

nodes.title: Fur Trade Thread Extended -- Ermine Adds the Royal Dimension
nodes.description: Beaver (61-62): fur trade shaped the continent. Mink (64): fur farming, invasive introductions. Fisher (66): trapped to local extinction. Weasel (67): ermine fur dressed royalty. Four fur trade species in the mammalian era, each with a different relationship to the fur trade. The weasel's ermine adds the CULTURAL dimension -- not just commerce but monarchy, heraldry, and judicial symbolism.

nodes.title: The Chris Walla Parallel
nodes.description: Both precise, efficient, and devastating relative to their size. Walla's production style is lean and exacting; the weasel kills with surgical precision at 200 grams. Both are departures: Walla departed Death Cab for Cutie, the weasel is the departed mustelid -- the fifth that breaks the quartet's inward spiral and moves to the edge. Both are understated forces achieving outsized impact. Walla E=3, Weasel E=4.

nodes.title: Spotted Owl Edge Connection (SPS-004)
nodes.description: Both are nocturnal predators of small mammals at the forest/edge interface. The Owl hunts from the forest side; the Weasel hunts from the meadow side. Complementary predation on the same prey base. Conservation contrast: the Owl is endangered, the Weasel is common. Two predators of similar prey, one threatened and one thriving -- resilience tracks habitat specificity.

nodes.title: Seventh Consecutive Original CSV Species
nodes.description: Degrees 61-67: seven consecutive original CSV species. The longest original-CSV streak in engine history extends again. Every mammalian entry has been an original CSV species. No substitutions needed or considered. The CSV's mammalian sequence is running clean and uncontested.

nodes.title: Eighth Mammalian Entry -- The Mammalian Era Extends
nodes.description: Eight mammalian entries across seven degrees (61-67). Six mammalian species: Beaver, River Otter, Mink, American Marten, Fisher, Short-tailed Weasel. Two orders (Rodentia, Carnivora), three families (Castoridae, Mustelidae). Mammalian representation rises to 11.8% (8 of 68 entries). The mammalian era is seven degrees deep. Now includes the first common, non-threatened mammalian species (Weasel).

nodes.title: Sixth Carnivore -- Still All Mustelidae
nodes.description: Carnivora now has six entries, all from Mustelidae: Otter (63), Mink (64), Marten (65), Fisher (66), Weasel (67). The order's entire mammalian representation comes from a single family. Six entries without a non-mustelid carnivore. When a canid, felid, ursid, or raptor eventually enters the engine, it will be Carnivora's first non-mustelid representative.

cross_references.relationship: CONSECUTIVE MUSTELIDAE. Quintet continues. Dense forest (Fisher) vs. meadow-forest edge (Weasel). Largest (2-6 kg) vs. smallest (150-350 g) terrestrial mustelids in the engine. The quintet's extremes: the Fisher goes deep into the forest; the Weasel breaks out to the edge. Specialist predation (Fisher, porcupines) vs. relentless generalist predation (Weasel, rodents).

MUSTELIDAE QUINTET continues. Both small, fierce, surplus killers. Similar acoustic profile (chitter). Creek bank vs. meadow edge. The quintet's behavioral mirror -- two small predators with overlapping prey and strategy but different metabolic pressures (Mink kills from opportunity, Weasel from necessity).

QUINTET ENDPOINTS (by size). River/estuary (Otter) vs. meadow-forest edge (Weasel). Social (Otter) vs. solitary (Weasel). The quintet's largest (5-14 kg) vs. smallest (150-350 g). A 40x size difference within one family. The five entries span three orders of magnitude in body mass.

BOTH SMALL MUSTELIDS. Old-growth canopy (Marten) vs. meadow edge (Weasel). Arboreal vs. terrestrial/subterranean. Two of the three smallest mustelids in the quintet. The Marten hunts from the canopy down; the Weasel hunts from the surface down into burrows. Vertical extremes within similar mass ranges.

BOTH EDGE/FOREST PREDATORS of small mammals. Both nocturnal. The Owl hunts from the forest side of the edge; the Weasel hunts from the meadow side. Complementary predation pressure on voles and mice. Conservation contrast: Owl endangered, Weasel thriving.
Eighth mammalian entry. Sixth mammalian species. Sixth Carnivora, fifth Mustelidae. Mammalian representation rises to 11.8% (8 of 68 entries). Two orders, three families, six species.

Meadow-forest edge habitat -- first edge-habitat species. Hypercarnivore with extreme metabolic demand (40-60% body weight daily). Surplus killer. Seasonal color change (ermine). Burrow pursuit specialist. Rodent population regulator. Climate-change vulnerability signal (ermine mismatch).
